turns.content: Hey, I've got my Axum handler set up with tower-http's Extractor to grab the request context. My current approach is just logging a static string as part of the span but it feels brittle. Is there a better way to handle this?

You're on the right track using tower-http, but instead of a static string, you should be generating a unique ID per request and propagating it through your tracing chain. The key is using the RequestId extractor to pull that value into your span context so every downstream service sees the same trace identifier.
Okay, so I should be creating an ID inside my middleware and then passing it along? And how do I make sure it survives across layers?

Exactly. You generate the RequestId in your first layer, then let tower-http's Extractor automatically attach that value to every span created downstream. This avoids manual context passing and keeps things declarative.
Got it. But what if I want to log the full request metadata with that ID? Should I still use static strings or can I attach dynamic data too?

You can mix both, but for anything variable like headers or body size, you should extract those values into your span using the Extractor trait. That way they're automatically scoped to that request and don't leak out.
That makes sense. So if I'm using axum's built-in logging, can I just call a simple function instead of writing custom code?
You can use the standard logger crate with axum, but for request IDs you really want to integrate tower-http's RequestId extractor so it works seamlessly with your tracing setup.
And does this mean I don't need to manually propagate anything across my middleware layers?
Right. Once you've set up the extractor in your first layer, tower-http handles the rest automatically for all downstream spans.
Wait, what if I want to add more context like user ID or session info? Can that still be done cleanly?
Yes. You can create custom extractors for those values and attach them alongside the RequestId in your span. The framework will handle propagation across layers automatically.
Okay, so this is basically just about setting up tower-http correctly? I think I get it now that static strings won't scale well for request tracing.

Exactly. The trick is letting the framework manage the context propagation instead of manually threading values around. You'll see a big difference in how your logs look once you switch to this approach.
I'm new to Rust and I want to add request tracing to an Axum service. How do I generate a unique request ID for every incoming HTTP request?

The best way is to extract the RequestId from the tower-http crate, which wraps your axum router and provides middleware hooks like Extractor. You can use a custom extractor that generates an uuid::Uuid or String ID on every request and attaches it via headers.
Once I have the ID, how do I ensure it propagates through downstream services if this Axum service calls another crate?

You should add a middleware that reads the X-Request-ID header from incoming requests and sets an environment variable or context field so that any downstream crates using tracing can see it. In Rust you might use tower-service to intercept responses and ensure headers are preserved.
What about logging? How do I make sure every log message includes this request ID automatically?

You can configure the tracing subscriber to extract context from your middleware and include it in every span. Using axum's built-in logging integration with tower-http allows you to attach fields like trace_id or request_id to all logs without manual parsing.
Can I use the standard library directly instead of a crate, or is there a reason to prefer a dedicated solution?

The standard library lacks built-in tracing hooks for HTTP layers. Using tower-http gives you structured middleware that integrates with axum cleanly and avoids reinventing boilerplate logic manually.
I see. But what if I need to support both async and sync code paths within the same service?

Since axum is fully async, you should design your middleware using tokio::sync or similar runtime-aware types so that request context stays consistent across all async blocks. The extractor pattern works uniformly regardless of sync vs async boundaries.
One more thing — what happens if a user sends an empty header instead of the X-Request-ID? Should I generate one automatically?

Yes, you should always fallback to generating a new ID when missing. This ensures every request has traceability even if clients omit headers. You can use an Option<String> in your extractor and default to uuid::Uuid::new_v4() if None is returned.
That makes sense. Is there a way to configure this globally so I don't repeat the same middleware setup across multiple routes?

You can wrap your entire router with a single tower-service layer that applies the extractor once at startup. This prevents duplication and ensures consistent behavior across all handlers without needing to modify each route individually.
